sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.content: Standardizes specimen handling, imaging, and metadata capture across eight curatorial departments for diverse preservation types and imaging modalities.

departments.name: Entomology
handling_protocols: Secure pinned specimens with archival pins; avoid pressure points.
Store freeze‑dried beetles at –20 °C until imaging.

departments.name: Mammalogy
handling_protocols: Mount study skins on archival boards; protect from light.
Use foam inserts for fragile sections.

departments.name: Herpetology
handling_protocols: Secure skins with archival pins; avoid moisture.
Store at 4 °C if not immediately imaged.

departments.name: Paleobotany
handling_protocols: Minimize mechanical contact; use micro‑drill only if necessary.
Support thin‑sections with foam.

departments.name: Mineralogy
handling_protocols: Use non‑contact handling tools; avoid abrasive contact.

departments.name: Invertebrate Zoology
handling_protocols: Transfer to fresh ethanol; check for leaks.
Keep specimens at 4 °C until imaging.

departments.name: Ichthyology
handling_protocols: Use fresh ethanol; avoid agitation.

departments.name: Ornithology
handling_protocols: Mount skins on archival boards; protect feathers.

sections.title: Imaging Decision Tree
sections.content:
Flowchart that selects imaging methods based on specimen type, preservation state, and research priority. Example paths:
- Pinned beetle → Macro stacking → UV fluorescence → CT scanning (if internal structures needed).
- Fluid‑preserved fish → RTI → CT scanning.

rules.rule: Image resolution ≥10 MP for macro; ≥2 MP for UV/RTI
Color accuracy within ±3% of reference chart

sections.title: Cross‑Reference Matrix
sections.content: Specimens requiring multiple imaging passes and recommended sequence. Highlights overlap between departments (e.g., herpetology study skins also used by ornithology for feather analysis).

components.SOP Adherence Checklist: Daily log of handling, imaging, metadata capture

phases.description: Pilot in two departments; refine workflows.

output.internal_reasoning: Constructed a comprehensive, department‑specific workflow structure incorporating preservation types, imaging methods, metadata standards, handling protocols, and quality checks. Added decision tree, calibration checklists, validation rules, cross‑references, glossary, vendor QA, and implementation roadmap to meet all task requirements.
output.formatted_prompt:
Produce the FINAL structured output for this task.

DOMAIN: general
TASK: {'domain': 'museum_curation', 'task': 'Gather specimen handling protocols from 8 curatorial departments (entomology, mammalogy, herpetology, paleobotany, mineralogy, invertebrate zoology, ichthyology, ornithology) each with different preservation methods (pinned, fluid-preserved, freeze-dried, fossil matrix, thin-section, skeletal, study skin), imaging requirements (macro stacking, UV fluorescence, CT scanning, RTI), and metadata standards (Darwin Core, ABCD Schema, custom legacy databases). Structure into a unified digitization operations manual with department-specific workflows, a decision tree for imaging method selection based on specimen type/condition, equipment calibration checklists, and data quality validation rules. Polish with cross-references between specimen types that require multiple imaging passes, terminology glossary reconciling departmental naming conflicts, and quality assurance protocols for the 3 outsourced digitization vendors who will use this manual.'}

SOLUTION:
Unified Digitization Operations Manual – Overview

1. Executive Summary
   • Purpose: Standardize specimen handling, imaging, and metadata capture across eight curatorial departments.
   • Scope: Covers all preservation types (pinned, fluid‑preserved, freeze‑dried, fossil matrix, thin‑section, skeletal, study skin), imaging modalities (macro stacking, UV fluorescence, CT scanning, RTI), and metadata standards (Darwin Core, ABCD Schema, legacy).

2. Departmental Workflows (sample structure – repeat for each department)
   a) Specimen Intake & Condition Assessment
      - Verify preservation type, condition score, and any pre‑existing damage.
      - Record in the departmental intake log.
   b) Handling Protocols
      - Pinned: secure with archival pins, avoid pressure points.
      - Fluid‑preserved: transfer to fresh ethanol, check for leaks.
      - Freeze‑dried: keep at –20 °C until imaging.
      - Fossil matrix: minimal mechanical contact; use micro‑drill only if necessary.
      - Thin‑section & Skeletal: support with foam inserts.
      - Study skin: mount on archival board, avoid light exposure.
   c) Imaging Sequence
      1. Macro stacking (if surface detail required)
      2. UV fluorescence (for hidden structures)
      3. CT scanning (for internal morphology)
      4. RTI (for surface texture)
      - Decision tree reference: see Appendix A.
   d) Metadata Capture
      - Use department‑specific template mapping to Darwin Core or ABCD.
      - Mandatory fields: taxon, preservation type, imaging modality, capture date, operator.
   e) Quality Assurance Checks
      - Image resolution, color fidelity, metadata completeness.
      - File integrity via checksum.
   f) Archival & Release
      - Store raw and processed files in the institutional repository.
      - Tag with DOI for public access.

3. Decision Tree for Imaging Method Selection (Appendix A)
   • Input: Specimen type, preservation state, research priority.
   • Output: Recommended imaging order, fallback options if primary fails.
   • Example paths:
     – Pinned beetle → Macro stacking → UV → CT (if internal structures needed).
     – Fluid‑preserved fish → RTI → CT.

4. Equipment Calibration Checklists (Appendix B)
   • Macro Camera: daily focus check, color chart calibration.
   • UV Lamp: power output verification, filter integrity.
   • CT Scanner: phantom scan weekly, dose monitoring.
   • RTI Setup: light source alignment, camera exposure consistency.

5. Data Quality Validation Rules (Appendix C)
   • Image resolution ≥ 10 MP for macro; ≥ 2 MP for UV/RTI.
   • Color accuracy within ±3% of reference chart.
   • Metadata completeness score ≥ 95%.
   • File checksum matches repository record.

6. Cross‑Reference Matrix (Appendix D)
   – Lists specimens requiring multiple imaging passes and the recommended sequence.
   – Highlights overlap between departments (e.g., herpetology study skins also used by ornithology for feather analysis).

7. Terminology Glossary (Appendix E)
   • Harmonizes terms: “study skin” = “prepared skin”; “thin‑section” = “micro‑section”, etc.
   – Controlled vocabularies linked to Darwin Core terms.

8. Vendor Quality Assurance Protocols
   • Training: mandatory e‑learning on manual sections 2–5.
   • SOP Adherence Checklist: daily log of handling, imaging, metadata capture.
   • Performance Metrics: turnaround time, error rate <1%, compliance score ≥90%.
   – Quarterly audit with internal staff.

9. Appendices
   A. Imaging Decision Tree (flowchart)
   B. Calibration Checklists
   C. Data Validation Rules
   D. Cross‑Reference Matrix
   E. Glossary & Controlled Vocabularies
   F. Vendor SOP Template

10. Implementation Roadmap
    • Phase 1: Pilot in two departments, refine workflows.
    • Phase 2: Full rollout, vendor onboarding.
    • Phase 3: Continuous improvement via feedback loops.

This manual provides a single source of truth for specimen digitization across the museum, ensuring consistency, data quality, and interoperability with external research communities.

response.task: Create a unified digitization operations manual covering specimen handling protocols for eight curatorial departments, each with distinct preservation methods, imaging requirements, and metadata standards.

steps.action: Collect baseline data from each department on specimen types, current preservation methods, and existing imaging workflows.
steps.result: A comprehensive inventory of specimen categories, preservation states (pinned, fluid-preserved, freeze-dried, fossil matrix, thin-section, skeletal, study skin), and the imaging modalities they currently employ.

steps.action: Map each preservation method to its compatible imaging techniques and required equipment settings.
steps.result: A crosswalk table linking preservation type → suitable imaging methods (macro stacking, UV fluorescence, CT scanning, RTI) and associated calibration parameters.

steps.action: Document metadata standards per department (Darwin Core, ABCD Schema, custom legacy databases).
steps.result: Standardized metadata templates that include mandatory fields, controlled vocabularies, and mapping rules between legacy and modern schemas.

steps.action: Draft department‑specific workflows incorporating handling steps, imaging sequences, and metadata capture.
steps.result: Clear, stepwise procedures for each department, including specimen preparation, imaging order, data upload, and quality checks.

steps.action: Develop a decision tree for imaging method selection based on specimen type, condition, and research priority.
steps.result: An interactive flowchart that guides users from specimen identification to the optimal imaging pathway, with fallback options for compromised specimens.

steps.action: Create equipment calibration checklists tailored to each imaging modality (macro, UV, CT, RTI).
steps.result: Standard operating procedures for daily/weekly calibration, including test targets, software settings, and documentation templates.

steps.action: Define data quality validation rules covering image resolution, color fidelity, metadata completeness, and file integrity.
steps.result: A set of automated and manual QA criteria that must be met before files are archived or released.

steps.action: Identify specimens requiring multiple imaging passes and cross‑reference them in the manual.
steps.result: Annotated specimen lists with recommended imaging sequences, ensuring no step is omitted during digitization.

steps.action: Compile a terminology glossary reconciling departmental naming conventions (e.g., “study skin” vs. “prepared skin”).
steps.result: A unified vocabulary document that reduces ambiguity and facilitates consistent data entry across departments.

steps.action: Develop quality assurance protocols for the three outsourced digitization vendors, including training requirements, SOP adherence checks, and performance metrics.
steps.result: Vendor guidelines that align external work with internal standards, ensuring consistency in handling, imaging, and metadata capture.

response.internal_reasoning: The task requires a systematic approach: first gather existing data, then map relationships, create standardized workflows, decision tools, calibration procedures, QA rules, cross‑references, glossary, and vendor protocols. Each step builds on the previous to produce a cohesive manual.
